company_description.value: Pre-seed B2B behavioral intelligence platform connecting wearable biometrics to individualized EWMA/CUSUM SPC baselines with a conversational AI agent

citations.content: The full context paragraph: "YU is a pre-seed B2B behavioral intelligence platform connecting wearable biometrics to individualized EWMA/CUSUM SPC baselines with a conversational AI agent"

parentBlock.content: Context: YU is a pre-seed B2B behavioral intelligence platform connecting wearable biometrics to individualized EWMA/CUSUM SPC baselines with a conversational Al agent. This report evaluates every major wearable API aggregation platform to identify the optimal multi-supplier architecture for scaling from 0 to 400 B2B users while maintaining HIPAA/SOC2 compliance and minimizing single-supplier dependency risk.

citations.content: The footer text 'Research conducted April 2026'

parentBlock.content: Research conducted April 2026. Pricing verified against official provider pages and third-party market analyses. Cost estimates marked "est." reflect market research benchmarks and competitor triangulation where official pricing was not publicly available.

report_scope.value: Covers 8 wearable API providers: Terra API, Spike API, Junction, Validic, ROOK, Thryve, Sahha, and Open Wearables. Includes provider profiles, compliance details, pricing, feature matrices, cost scenarios, financial stability, recommended architecture for YU, compliance roadmap, final scoring, and key insights.

citations.content: The context paragraph describing the scope of the document

parentBlock.content: Context: YU is a pre-seed B2B behavioral intelligence platform connecting wearable biometrics to individualized EWMA/CUSUM SPC baselines with a conversational Al agent. This report evaluates every major wearable API aggregation platform to identify the optimal multi-supplier architecture for scaling from 0 to 400 B2B users while maintaining HIPAA/SOC2 compliance and minimizing single-supplier dependency risk.

primary_recommendation.value: Terra API as primary provider at $399/month

citations.content: The text referencing Terra as primary recommendation at '$399/month' in executive summary paragraph 1

parentBlock.content: The wearable API aggregation market in 2026 is maturing but remains fragmented, with seven primary players each occupying distinct positioning niches. Terra API remains YU's best primary provider, backed by YC pedigree, SOC 2 Type II + HIPAA + GDPR compliance, the most transparent pricing on the market, and 99% wearable coverage - all at $399/month for up to ~500 users on an annual plan. At 400 users, Terra costs roughly $399/month, the lowest absolute cost among fully compliant providers.

recommended_architecture.value: Dual-primary architecture: Terra as the default provider with Spike API as a parallel secondary, a Junction contract for lab + US healthcare coverage, and an Open Wearables self-hosted layer for Apple Health and Garmin data as an insurance hedge

citations.content: The text describing dual-primary architecture with Terra, Spike API, Junction, and Open Wearables self-hosted layer

parentBlock.content: However, with only $2.8M in total funding raised in 2021 and no public follow-on rounds, Terra carries non-trivial financial stability risk for a startup that cannot tolerate a critical supplier shutdown. The strategic recommendation for YU is a dual-primary architecture: Terra as the default provider with Spike API as a parallel secondary, a Junction contract for lab + US healthcare coverage, and an Open Wearables self-hosted layer for Apple Health and Garmin data as an insurance hedge. This structure reduces single-supplier dependency from ~100% to below 40% on any one vendor.

risks_for_yu.value: Financial stability risk (no follow-on funding in 5 years); BAA is enterprise-only (not available on Quick Start plan); Consumer-fitness DNA may not align with YU's health use case; Data quality concerns for clinical applications

citations.content: Terra financial stability risk block

parentBlock.content: • Financial stability risk: Only $2.8M raised in 2021; no public follow-on funding rounds-this is the biggest red flag. If Terra shuts down or is acquired by a competitor, YU loses its core data layer overnight.

citations.content: Terra BAA enterprise-only risk block

parentBlock.content: ⚫ BAA is Enterprise-only: HIPAA's BAA requirement means YU cannot be fully HIPAA-compliant with its own corporate customers until it upgrades to Enterprise pricing (unknown cost).

citations.content: Terra consumer-fitness DNA risk block

parentBlock.content: • Consumer-fitness DNA: Terra is optimized for fitness tracking, not clinical/healthcare-grade continuous monitoring. Data normalization and gap-handling are left to the client —this increases YU's engineering burden for the SPC pipeline.

citations.content: Terra API data quality risk block

parentBlock.content: • API data quality: Limited to what each wearable manufacturer exposes; no active harmonization or deduplication across sources (if a user has Apple Watch AND Garmin, Terra returns two separate streams requiring YU to reconcile).

technical_features.value: MCP (Model Context Protocol) layer for LLM integration; Raw and normalized data formats; Dedicated engineer support; Medical IoT support (Dexcom, ECG, BP); No data storage architecture; AI analytics capabilities

citations.content: Spike MCP layer block

parentBlock.content: • MCP (Model Context Protocol) layer: Spike offers a native MCP integration that makes wearable data directly compatible with LLMS without preprocessing - this is strategically critical for YU's conversational AI agent. Spike-sourced data can pipe directly into YU's SPC engine and agent layer with minimal transformation.

citations.content: Spike raw+normalized formats block

parentBlock.content: • Raw + normalized formats: Both unified normalized schema and device-specific raw data available simultaneously-ideal for YU's individualized baselining that needs exact per-device granularity.

citations.content: Spike dedicated engineer block

parentBlock.content: • Dedicated implementation engineer: Every client gets a named engineer for onboarding ― dramatically reduces integration time.

citations.content: Spike medical IoT block

parentBlock.content: • Medical IoT coverage: Dexcom glucose, blood pressure monitors, ECG devices-expands YU's addressable market into clinical populations (insurance, occupational health).

citations.content: Spike no data storage differentiator block

parentBlock.content: • No data storage by Spike: Satisfies privacy-first enterprise buyers (financial services).

citations.content: Spike AI analytics block

parentBlock.content: • Built for AI analytics: Structured, model-compatible data pipelines reduce YU's preprocessing cost for CUSUM/EWMA calculations.

unique_differentiators.value: Deep harmonization of wearable data for healthcare-grade analysis; 24/7 continuous data streaming capability; Statistical process control (SPC) analysis for clinical insights; Positioned as healthcare upgrade from Terra

citations.content: ROOK strategic importance for YU block

parentBlock.content: Why ROOK is Strategically Important for YU: YU's SPC engine is only as good as the data it receives. If a user's WHOOP data has a gap on Day 3 and ROOK fills it with a validated imputation while Terra returns a null, YU's CUSUM chart fires a false alert on the Terra feed. ROOK's data quality focus directly reduces YU's false positive rate - the metric that determines whether enterprise buyers renew. ROOK should be evaluated as a parallel integration for YU's highest-value pilot accounts.

citations.content: Sahha critical limitation block

parentBlock.content: Critical Limitation for YU: Sahha's coverage is limited to Apple Health and Google Health Connect― it cannot connect to Garmin, WHOOP, Oura, or any direct wearable APIs. This means users with non- iOS/Android-native devices cannot connect. For YU's target market (PE/finance professionals and corporate employees wearing WHOOP and Garmin), Sahha's coverage gap is disqualifying as a primary provider. However, its pre-built behavioral scoring could supplement YU's own SPC layer for Apple Watch users.

citations.content: Core principle of abstraction layer block

parentBlock.content: The core principle: build a provider-agnostic abstraction layer internally so that any single provider can be swapped in 2-4 weeks of engineering effort, not 3 months. This is the most important architectural decision YU can make in 2026.

abstraction_layer_description.value: The WearableDataBus interface acts as a unified schema between YU's app and all wearable providers. With this schema, swapping providers requires zero application-level changes.

citations.content: The Abstraction Layer (Critical Engineering Priority) header

citations.content: Build WearableDataBus interface block

parentBlock.content: Build a WearableDataBus interface in YU's backend with these properties:

citations.content: With this schema text block

parentBlock.content: With this schema, YU's SPC engine (EWMA/CUSUM) never talks directly to Terra — it always consumes from the Wearable DataBus. When Terra is down, the bus routes to Spike. When both are down, Open Wearables serves Apple/Garmin data for the subset of users on those devices. This design costs ~3-4 weeks of engineering time but eliminates single-supplier existential risk permanently.

wearable_data_bus_schema.value: Interface WearableDataBus { getUserActivity(userId, dateRange): ActivityData; getUserSleep(userId, dateRange): SleepData; getUserHeartRate(userId, dateRange): HRData; streamRealTime(userId, callback): void; }

citations.content: WearableDataBus schema definition block

parentBlock.content:
WearableDataBus {
    provider_id: string // "terra" | "spike" | "junction" | "openwearables"
    user_id: string
    device_type: string
    metric_type: HRV | sleep_stages | RHR | activity | recovery
    raw_value: any
    normalized_value: NormalizedHealthMetric
    timestamp: ISO8601
    confidence_score: float // provider-reported data quality
    provider_fallback: string // which tier served this record
}

parentBlock.content: The Non-Obvious HIPAA Point: Terra's BAA is Enterprise plan only. key_non_obvious_insights.value: Insight 1: Terra BAA gap - Terra's BAA is only available on enterprise plans, meaning Quick Start users at $399/month technically cannot sign a BAA, creating a HIPAA compliance gap for early-stage YU
Insight 2: Spike MCP layer - Spike is the only provider with a native Model Context Protocol (MCP) layer, making it uniquely positioned for YU's conversational AI agent use case
Insight 3: 100k Terra credit limit - Terra's Quick Start includes 100,000 credits/month; at typical usage rates this supports roughly 100-200 active users before overages kick in
Insight 4: Garmin API partnership - Garmin has direct API partnerships that make Open Wearables + Garmin a viable 60-70% coverage solution without any third-party aggregator
Insight 5: Open Wearables launched December 2025 - This is a very new open-source project; YU would be an early adopter with both opportunity and risk

Insight 6: Market consolidation risk - LexisNexis acquired Human API in 2023, signaling that wearable API aggregators are acquisition targets; Terra or Spike could be acquired and pivot away from startup-friendly pricing

acquisition_risk_vector.value: LexisNexis Risk Solutions acquired Human API in 2023, demonstrating that wearable API aggregators are acquisition targets. A similar acquisition of Terra or Spike could result in pricing changes, strategic pivots away from startup customers, or product discontinuation. This is a key rationale for YU's multi-supplier strategy.

citations.content: LexisNexis acquired Human API block

parentBlock.content: LexisNexis acquired Human API in March 2023-changing its focus from developer-friendly to insurance underwriting. This is the exact failure mode: a provider is acquired by a large incumbent, the developer API is defunded or repriced, and startups dependent on it must emergency-migrate. YU should assume this scenario is possible for Terra or Spike within a 24-month window.
